The building materials sector was a notable detractor from performance relative to the benchmark. Within this sector, Cornerstone Building Brands was softer in sympathy with weak earnings from a competitor and continued to be under pressure despite a lack of other news. The consumer products sector, including MajorDrive Holdings, was also unfavourable. Conversely, unsecured bonds and especially our exposure to Pemex contributed to performance. The chemical sector, including Consolidated Energy Finance and the gaming sector, including Affinity Gaming, were also beneficial. While Affinity Gaming has been volatile for several months, we maintain our conviction in the regional gaming operator.

In terms of ratings, non-rated and lower quality CCC-rated bonds weighed on performance. Conversely, BB and B-rated bonds were favourable.

Market review

The global high-yield asset class ended the first quarter slightly higher.

High-yield assets started the year off on a positive note as the combination of tighter corporate bond spreads and lower government bond yields were favorable for returns. With President Trump taking office in January, there was no shortage of market-moving daily headlines, as investors looked to make sense of the potential impact on corporates from the new administration. After testing cycle lows during January, credit spreads widened out marginally into month end.

As the quarter progressed, the political headline barrage continued in the US, leading to volatility around both rates and spreads. While markets appeared to be numb to the rhetoric from the White House, economic data started to show traces of weakness in February. This led to falling rates in the US as markets increasingly priced in more interest rate cuts from the Federal Reserve (Fed). Investors in search of clarity on the intentions of the Trump administration’s tariff policy were left unsatisfied in March, with the resulting uncertainty reflected in high-yield credit spreads. Throughout the month, investors looked to rotate into higher quality holdings in an effort to become more defensive.

Outlook & strategy

Uncertainty has gripped financial markets and the passage of time has done little to clear the fog that surrounds the White House’s ultimate intentions. Corporates and consumers alike are attempting to sort through the noise, but the collateral damage to confidence in all parts of the economy has had a noticeable impact on markets. While hard data on the economic front has held up well so far, the focus is clearly on where we go from here, as the environment has changed, throwing the efficacy of past economic data and prior-quarter corporate earnings into question. Forward-looking guidance from corporate management teams should be the focus from here, but that too will likely be clouded by uncertainty, leaving investors with little in the way of tangible evidence to point to, as we navigate financial markets going forward.

Fortunately, within the high-yield market, corporate fundamentals are in a position to weather a storm, should one continue to materialise. Maturity runways have been cleared, liquidity is strong, and from a credit ratings perspective, the market is higher in quality than it has been historically. Valuations have backed up and are offering compelling investment cases for credits that have the ability to withstand a recession. While we expect that uncertainty will continue to define the investment backdrop over the coming months, we are seeing attractive opportunities within our market that could reward the patient investor.

data.title: AK John Hancock Freedom 529 Portfolio 2033-2036 Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary1 Highlights

  • In the first quarter of 2025, the bellwether S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) recorded a mid-single-digit decline after posting yet another fresh all-time high in mid-February.
  • Postelection optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, especially related to trade and tariffs.
  • The Fed kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024.

Market review and outlook

In the first quarter of 2025, the bellwether S&P 500 Index recorded a mid-single digit decline after posting yet another fresh all-time high in mid-February. Post election optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, disruption to government programs, and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities, especially related to trade and tariffs. Amid rising volatility, the index gained 2.78% in January before falling in February (−1.30%) and March (-5.63%). It was particularly dragged down by growth stocks and small-cap stocks.

During the quarter, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024. The central bank indicated that further reductions would depend on real progress on inflation or unexpected weakness in labor. New data showed that inflation climbed 2.80% in February from a year earlier-higher than the Fed's 2.00% target-as underlying price pressure intensified. Meanwhile, confidence and sentiment among U.S. consumers continued to slip, mostly reflecting a gloomier outlook for inflation and the economy's path ahead.

Against this backdrop, the S&P 500 Index returned -4.27% in the first quarter. However, international developed-market stocks gained 6.94%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index, buoyed partly by strength in Germany (+16.00%), where February brought elections and optimism that the new administration led by Friedrich Merz would pursue a pro-growth agenda. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 2.93% advance. Some emerging European markets, including Poland (+31%), Greece (+23%), and the Czech Republic (+29%), posted strong returns, bolstered by an improved outlook for the eurozone following Germany's plans to boost spending on defense and infrastructure. China (+15%) was also a standout performer; however, Taiwan (-13%) and India (-3.00%) were relatively weak.

Investment-grade bonds rebounded during the quarter as U.S. Treasury yields eased. Long-term Treasuries and Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities did particularly well amid growing economic growth and inflation concerns. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index rose 2.78%.

Almost every measure of consumer and business sentiment, or soft data, has notably deteriorated amid rising uncertainty around trade policy. While sentiment usually lags hard data, such as employment and spending, today's soft data signals a far more dire outlook than the hard data implies. The real concern is whether businesses and consumers will continue to face this level of uncertainty for a prolonged period. With trade disputes lasting for nearly a year and a half during President Trump's first term, the sentiment could be right this time around, foreshadowing a deeper economic slowdown. Given the heightened risk, we continued to lower U.S. equity exposure while adding to European equities on an upside potential related to Germany's increased fiscal spending on defense and infrastructure and a more unified commitment from leaders across the eurozone on improving competitiveness.

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio (MUTF:JENFX) recorded a slight gain for the quarter and outpaced its blended benchmark, comprising a 53.37% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index, a 22.88% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net), and a 23.75% weighting in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. An out-ofbenchmark stake in the Real Assets Fund (T. Rowe Price) notably contributed for the quarter, as this asset class outperformed core equities, driven by the precious metals and energy groups. Exposure to non-U.S. stocks also added value in a period during which U.S. equities lagged. International Growth Fund (John Hancock) was a key relative contributor, aided by security selection in the industrials sector. Equity Income Fund (T. Rowe Price) also provided a relative performance boost, largely on the strength of security selection within industrials and consumer staples. On the other hand, Disciplined Value Fund (John Hancock) detracted, mainly due to stock selection in information technology, communication services, and financials.

data.title: abrdn Healthcare Opportunities Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

The equity portion of the Fund rose (gross of fees) but underperformed its benchmark over the quarter.[1]

The top three detractors were Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT), Merck (MRK), and Tandem Diabetes Care (TNDM). Sarepta underperformed after reporting a patient death due to acute liver failure linked to its lead gene therapy product, Elevidys, for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy — a development that raised significant investor concern. Merck declined after news that it would slow shipments of its Gardasil vaccine to China in support of a local partner’s inventory reduction efforts, which led to a sales decline during the fourth quarter of 2024. Tandem Diabetes Care fell after posting fourth-quarter earnings that missed analyst expectations, reflecting intensified competition in the insulin pump market.

The top three positive contributors were AbbVie (ABBV), Abbott Laboratories (ABT) and Eli Lilly (LLY). AbbVie rose on the back of stronger-than-expected fourth-quarter earnings, with continued growth in immunology drugs Skyrizi and Rinvoq offsetting declining Humira sales. Abbott gained after delivering robust fourth-quarter earnings and forward guidance, while investor concerns surrounding litigation tied to its infant formula operations began to ease. Eli Lilly outperformed the broader healthcare sector, supported by weaker-than-expected clinical data from competitor Novo Nordisk in the increasingly competitive obesity and diabetes markets.

The Fund is subject to investment risk, including the possible loss of principal. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized.

Activity

During the first quarter of 2025, we made several changes to the portfolio. Generally speaking, the portfolio management team identified relative value differentials favoring smaller-cap equities over large-cap pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies. Additionally, concerns over pending tariffs led us to adopt a more cautious stance toward the large-cap pharmaceutical and biotechnology sector. Consequently, we initiated several positions in predominantly small- and mid-cap pre-commercial or early product launch companies. Examples of new initiations included Neurocrine Biosciences (NBIX), Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR), NeoGenomics (NEO), ARS Pharmaceuticals (SPRY), and Sera Prognostics (SERA). We also made a new investment in a pre-public, venture-backed company, Abcuro.

We sold several positions for various reasons. Some sales were motivated by a lack of confidence in the companies’ forward growth prospects, while some were as a result of share-price appreciation relative to broader healthcare equities. Examples of sold positions include Avantor (AVTR), argenx (ARGX), and Tempus AI (TEM).

Market review

US equities ended the quarter notably down. Against a backdrop of lingering inflationary pressures and a relatively buoyant labour market, the US Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained the target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25–4.50% during the quarter. The central bank’s chair, Jerome Powell, reiterated the Fed’s commitment to sustainably lowering annual inflation – as measured by its preferred measure, the core Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index – below the 2% target. However, the Fed aims to proceed cautiously, taking a data-dependent approach as it seeks greater clarity on underlying economic trends given persistently high inflation data and the potential inflationary impact of President Trump’s policies, particularly his tariff announcements. Despite this, following some softer US economic data, investors are now factoring in the possibility that rates could be cut at a slightly faster pace than previously expected.

The performance of the US healthcare sector notably outpaced that of the S&P 500 Index over the quarter. This was due to the relatively defensive nature of healthcare stocks, along with other sectors such as utilities and real estate that also outperformed. Investor risk aversion increased amid concerns that President Trump’s recent tariff announcements could prove inflationary and weigh on US economic growth. There were also heightened geopolitical tensions due to the ongoing wars in Ukraine and the Middle East. The energy sector outperformed too, supported by its perceived role as an inflation hedge, the Trump administration’s ‘energy dominance’ policy, and heightened geopolitical tensions. Materials also fared well, as fears of a potential trade war and geopolitical uncertainty supported gold prices. Elsewhere, the consumer discretionary sector was the main laggard, reflecting mounting concerns about the health of the US consumer. Information technology also notably underperformed, as the prospect of interest rates staying higher for longer weighed on the sector, given its large weighting to growth companies whose future discounted earnings would be negatively affected.

Outlook & strategy

With Donald Trump securing his second term, uncertainty around election results has been replaced with policy ambiguity. Tax cuts and deregulation are supportive of GDP growth, but incremental tariffs and immigration restrictions could mute these gains. This combination of looser fiscal policy coupled with potential supply-side shocks raises inflation expectations. As a result, we expect Fed rate cuts to slow in 2025 while we await increased visibility over the policy agenda and its resulting impact on inflation.

In general, the near- and long-term outlooks for healthcare companies remain favourable. Long-term demographic trends of an aging population should continue to support the growing demand for new healthcare products and therapies. Due to the healthcare sector’s generally defensive characteristics, its relative underperformance in the short term should position it favourably, especially if the US and global economies slow down.

data.title: German Inflation Edges Closer To ECB's 2% Benchmark
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

The just-released flash estimate of German inflation for May brought some more relief for the European Central Bank as headline inflation remained unchanged at 2.1% year-on-year, while the European inflation measure dropped to 2.1% YoY from 2.2% YoY in April.

Core inflation came down somewhat to 2.8% YoY, from 2.9% in April. It’s not a big move, but the process of gradual disinflation continues.

Inflation is trending lower and should now hover around 2% until year-end

The available regional state data suggests that falling monthly prices for food, clothing and transportation – as well as lower heating oil and gasoline prices compared to last year – pushed down headline inflation. Meanwhile, services inflation remained broadly unchanged.

It appears that US President Donald Trump not only made the German economy great again – at least for one quarter, as frontloading effects boosted the economy – but also played an important role in bringing down inflation. The drop in oil prices, as well as the stronger euro exchange rate, have added to disinflationary pressures in Germany, and the entire eurozone.

Unrelated to US trade policies, the softening German labour market is also exerting downward pressure on inflation. Although inflation expectations have recently ticked up, we interpret this as a misreading – attributing inflationary effects to current trade tensions, which are unlikely to materialise as long as Europe refrains from retaliation – rather than as a sign of mounting inflationary concerns.

Looking ahead, at least in the nearer term, German inflation is likely to continue its downward trend, probably dropping below 2% over the coming months. More structurally speaking, two opposing trends will shape the future path of inflation. On the one hand, the cooling of the labour market should take away wage pressures and consequently inflationary pressures; on the other hand, the government’s fiscal stimulus is likely to push up inflationary pressure towards the end of the year and beyond.

As a result, and based on lower energy prices, we now expect German headline inflation to hover around 2% throughout the second half of the year.

ECB looks set to cut rates again next week

For the ECB, today’s German inflation reading should bring further relief: the process of gradual disinflation is continuing. Even if the latest developments in the trade and tariff saga have somewhat strengthened the case for a pause next week, arguments for a rate cut are much stronger.

The expected downward revision of the inflation forecasts and a much earlier drop in headline inflation to below 2%, alongside the rising risk of inflation undershooting, should tilt the balance towards a 25bp rate cut. It is another rate cut that can be titled as an insurance or risk management cut. It won’t do any harm if the economy continues to prove resilient or trade tensions fade away, and it will have been exactly the right decision if resilience falters.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cohu: What The Latest Investor Presentation Says About The Outlook
data.description:
Cohu (COHU), a supplier of test, automation, inspection and metrology products and services to the semiconductor industry, has managed to put together a rebound off the lows, but there is no denying the large and extended decline in the stock, especially after mid-2023. The decline has been fueled by an extended downturn in demand that has already lasted several years, but COHU is confident it can do much better in the coming years, as shown in the latest Investor Presentation. Why will be covered next.

The Trend Is Still Leaning Bearish

A previous article from November 2024 was written when the stock was in the midst of a strong move higher. The rally was assisted by an upbeat outlook, which called for a return to growth after a downturn. This offset the fact that quarterly results and guidance had fallen short, but the article concluded that restraint was in order since the stock was still in an overall downtrend, despite the short-term rally in the stock. The article therefore decided to rate COHU a hold.

The chart above shows how the stock was able to keep it going in the rest of 2024, but the floor fell out in 2025 and the stock resumed its decline for various reasons, including the prospect of trade conflicts due to tariffs. The decline eventually resulted in a new 52-weeks low of $12.57 in early April, but the stock has moved higher in recent weeks thanks to a rally in the stock market after the U.S. government eased up on tariffs.

However, the stock is still down 36.5% YTD after closing at $16.96. Furthermore, not only has the stock done poorly, this poor performance goes back quite some time as shown in the chart. Note, for instance, how the stock has followed the lower trendline on the way down, not something you would like to see. The stock even fell below the trendline, and it failed trying to get back above it. While COHU has been negatively affected by recent trade tensions, the stock’s decline in the last couple of years has been primarily powered by an ongoing downturn in demand, which has found its way to quarterly results.

COHU Remains In A Downturn, But Maybe Not For Much Longer

This downturn has yet to end. In the most recent or Q1 FY2025 report, for instance, COHU reported a GAAP loss of $0.66 a share, and a non-GAAP loss of $0.02 a share, on revenue of $96.8M. In comparison, COHU reported a GAAP loss of $0.31 a share, and a non-GAAP profit of $0.01 a share, on revenue of $107.6M in Q1 FY2024. The numbers got significantly worse in the most recent report.

Note that Q1 FY2025 includes contributions from Tignis, which was acquired in January for $34.9M in cash. As a consequence, the balance sheet shows a deterioration with cash and investments falling to $200.8M at the end of Q1 FY2025, down from $262.1M in Q4 FY2024, partially offset by total debt of $31.8M.

The GAAP loss of $0.66 a share is actually the largest on record since FY2018. The YoY decline in Q1 FY2025 revenue also made it the 14th consecutive quarter to contract, which gives one a sense of the extent of the downturn COHU has experienced. During this time, revenue shrank from a record $887M in FY2021 to $402M in FY2024, which is less than half. In turn, GAAP EPS collapsed from a profit of $3.45 in FY2021 to a loss of $1.49 in FY2024.

COHU has been in a downturn for years and this is reflected in the low valuations assigned to it. For instance, COHU has a book value of $827.7M with total assets of $975.5M and total liabilities of $147.8M, which converts to a book value of $17.80 a share since the total number of outstanding shares is 46.5M. COHU can thus be had at less than book value with a price-to-book of 0.95x and the stock priced at $16.96 a share.

Could The Bottom Be In For COHU?

However, COHU believes an end to the downturn is in sight and there are some signs that point in this direction. Q1 FY2025 revenue, for instance, increased QoQ, which is the first time this has happened after 10 consecutive QoQ declines in revenue. This is expected to continue with guidance calling for revenue of $99-113M in Q2 FY2025, up from $97M in the preceding quarter. The midpoint, or $106M, also represents a YoY increase versus $105M in Q2 FY2024, which would make it the first YoY increase after 14 straight quarterly contractions.

COHU was also optimistic about its prospects for the rest of FY2025. While COHU remains cautious, it expects FY2025 revenue to increase YoY, which would make it the first time for this to happen after three consecutive years of YoY decreases. From the Q1 FY2025 earnings call:

“With that said, I think we are all in this space a little cautious right now in trying to understand, look, we haven’t really seen any meaningful change in customer buying pattern due to tariffs. With that said, I think we are cautious and we want to remain cautious and not get ahead of ourselves. So, we are not going to say a ton about third quarter and beyond other than to say we still expect this to be a year-over-year growth year for Cohu. And at this point, we feel that consensus estimates for us are in a good place for the second half of the year and we are going to leave it at that until we gain more visibility into what’s happening.”

Source: COHU earnings call

What The Outlook Suggests According To The Latest Financial Model

COHU seems to think an upturn after the long downturn is not far away. The optimism is reflected in the most recent Investor Presentation held last week, which included, among other things, the latest financial model as COHU sees it. This model, using FY2024 as the base, targets revenue of $600-800M, a non-GAAP gross margin of 48-50% and non-GAAP EPS of $1.50-3.25, all in the next 3-5 years or FY2027-2029.

In comparison, COHU posted a non-GAAP loss of $0.23 a share on revenue of $402M with a gross margin of 45% in FY2024. The model suggests revenue will grow at a double-digit CAGR of around 14% in the next several years, a sharp reversal from the contraction in revenue of the past three years mentioned earlier, which saw revenue fall to less than half in FY2021-2024.

The model also suggests FY2025 non-GAAP EPS will get out of the red, probably ending at an estimated $0.10 a share. At the current share price of $16.96, this implies a P/E ratio of 169.6x. This is a very high multiple, which may turn off those who are more interested in stocks trading at lower multiples. On the other hand, a high multiple can be worth it if earnings grow fast enough.

If we assume COHU grows earnings in line with what the model suggests, say $2.40 by FY2028, then COHU would need to grow earnings by about 188.5%, on average, per year in order for non-GAAP EPS to grow from $0.10 in FY2025 to $2.40 in FY2028. This is not impossible, but the bar is set very high with such a fast increase.

Nonetheless, the above implies a PEG of 0.9x with a P/E of 169.6x and earnings growing at a CAGR of 188.5%. According to the methodology promoted by people like Peter Lynch and others, COHU could be somewhat undervalued relative to growth for this reason. Fair value would be about $18.85, assuming the estimated FY2025 EPS of $0.10 is correct and earnings grow at a CAGR of 188.5% in FY2025-2028. This is above the current share price of $16.96.

The caveat here that needs to be mentioned is that in order for the above to hold true, earnings need to grow at a very fast pace in the coming years. This is not an easy task, especially with all the uncertainty surrounding global trade and the effect that could have on global growth and semiconductor demand by extension. The risk that COHU does not grow as envisioned in the model is there. If earnings grow at a significantly slower pace, if at all, then COHU could wind up with a PEG above one and probably not worth buying at current prices.

Investor Takeaways

COHU has been in a downturn for quite some time. The latest report included the biggest quarterly GAAP net loss in seven years after revenue shrank YoY for the 14th consecutive quarter. The slump in demand, together with the recent turmoil on the trade front caused the stock price to fall to lows not seen since the depths of the COVID-19 crisis in early 2020. The stock price has moved higher in recent weeks, thanks to the lowering of trade tensions, but COHU is still valued at slightly less than book value.

It may be tempting to short COHU since that has been a winning play for the last couple of years, and with patterns in the charts leaning bearish, but this is becoming an increasingly dicey proposition over time because the downturn has already lasted so long. It is very hard to see the downturn continuing indefinitely. At some point, the downturn has to end and if the latest outlook from COHU is accurate, the end of the downturn is close by.

Guidance calls for the first QoQ and YoY increase in revenue in years in Q2 FY2025, which could be the start of an upturn. The FY2025 outlook calls for the first year of growth after years of contraction. Furthermore, a very recent financial model calls for the top and the bottom line to grow very fast in the next 3-5 years, another sign COHU feels the downturn is over.

However, the goals laid out are very ambitious, perhaps even too ambitious. This raises the possibility COHU may come up short, especially since COHU is still dealing with lots of uncertainty on the global stage. This could lead to disruptions, throwing all models into disarray. According to the methodology used to evaluate stocks from Peter Lynch, COHU needs to come close to tripling earnings per year, or significantly more than the 169.6% calculated earlier, for PEG to come in below one and to be worth buying, which is very high. Granted, the base has been lowered a great deal due to the length of the downturn, which makes it easier to make big gains, but this still looks like a lot to ask for, especially given the overall environment stocks like COHU find themselves in at this time.

Keep in mind that financial models do not always pan out. COHU has been optimistic before about the outlook, only for this optimism to turn out to be misplaced. While COHU may have a decent shot at ending the downturn, if only because the downturn has already lasted such a long time, COHU’s chances to grow as envisioned according to the model should probably be treated with some doubt.

I am therefore neutral on COHU. Shorting COHU has been a winning move for a long time, but it looks risky at this point after all the beating COHU has taken in recent years, both in terms of earnings and the stock price itself. It’s not impossible for the stock to move down again, but to be still shorting at this point is fraught with risk.

On the other hand, while there are positive signs the long downturn is coming to an end and the stock’s asking price looks interesting in some ways with COHU available at less than book value, the case for long COHU is also fraught with risk. COHU needs to grow earnings at a very fast pace, which means the bar has been set very high. It also means the odds COHU does not cross the bar are likely also high. If earnings grow at a more modest pace than aimed for, which is probably the more likely outcome, then COHU is likely not worth betting on at current levels.

Bottom line, both the bear and the bull case for COHU have their drawbacks. The latter could make a better case if COHU shows that the earnings growth aimed for is absolutely achievable. It would also help if the uncertainty surrounding trade policy is lowered since that would improve the odds of success for the latest financial model. This has yet to happen, which is why standing on the sidelines is arguably best for now.

data.title: Host Hotels & Resorts: Fortress Balance Sheet, 5% Yield Make This A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

I have written about several hospitality REITs in recent weeks. Similarly, the focus of this article, Host Hotels & Resorts (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:HST), is a hospitality REIT. When I last covered the company in 2023, I had a “Buy” rating on the company.

With the release of its Q1 2025 earnings a month ago, I re-evaluated the company in this article. I maintain my “Buy” rating on the company, as I believe the company’s strong balance sheet and stable dividend offer an attractive risk-reward profile for income-oriented investors.

Background

With a market capitalization of around $10.7 billion, Host Hotels & Resorts is by far the largest publicly traded hospitality REIT. To give some context, the next largest publicly traded hospitality REIT is Ryman Hospitality Properties (NYSE: RHP), with a market capitalization of approximately $6 billion.

The company has a substantial footprint in the hospitality landscape, with 43,400 rooms in 81 hotels. While the company has a small international presence, with 5 hotels (and 1,499 rooms) located in Brazil and Canada, the bulk of its presence is in the United States. The company’s focus is on luxury and upper upscale hotels, with 97% of its rooms positioned within this category. These hotels mainly operate under well-known brands such as Marriott and Hyatt, allowing the company to capitalize on the prestige associated with these names.

With such a large number of hotels, the company’s portfolio is geographically diversified, with no single market accounting for more than 9% of the company’s revenue. This ensures the company is well protected against any regional disruptions, which in turn allows for greater stability.

Balance Sheet

Among lodging REITs, Host Hotels & Resorts holds the distinction of being the only investment-grade credit-rated REIT. This is, of course, largely due to the company’s balance sheet.

As of Q1 2025, the company has a total available liquidity of $2.2 billion, which includes $1.5 billion under its credit facility. Moreover, virtually all of the company’s portfolio is unencumbered by debt. Looking at the company’s debt profile, the company has a total debt of approximately $5 billion, with a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 2.8x. The weighted average interest rate on its debt is 4.7%, with a weighted average maturity of 5 years.

All in all, the company’s substantial liquidity coupled with its well-balanced maturity schedule means that the company possesses the flexibility to withstand any economic shocks, as well as pursue any potential opportunities in the market.

Dividend

From 2015 to 2019, prior to the pandemic, the company had been consistently paying a quarterly dividend of $0.20/share. Similar to other REITs, the company halted its dividends in 2020 as a result of the pandemic and only resumed them in 2022 at a rate of $0.03/share. However, the company increased its dividends in subsequent quarters, and by the time I wrote about the company in 2023, its dividends had recently been increased to $0.15/share. At that time, I stated that “the company may very well increase its dividends in the near future”, due to its strong balance sheet and low payout ratio. The company proved me right, increasing its dividends in the next 2 quarters, first to $0.18/share and then back to $0.20/share, effectively restoring it to pre-pandemic levels, where it has remained since.

At the current quarterly rate of $0.20/share, the company’s annualized dividend stands at $0.80/share. With a current share price of $15.42, this represents a very respectable dividend yield of 5.19%. Crucially, this figure excludes any special dividends paid by the company. To give some context, the company paid a $0.10/share special dividend in 2024 and had also done so regularly in the years prior to the pandemic.

In its latest Q1 2025 results, management raised its full-year 2025 AFFO guidance to between $1.88/share and $1.97/share. Even at the low end of guidance, this implies a dividend payout ratio of approximately 42%; even with the $0.10/share special dividend included, the payout ratio still remains comfortably under 50%. In other words, the company is able to cover its dividends comfortably.

However, it should be noted that while the company does have room to increase its dividends, I do not expect another increase in the near future. The company has returned to its pre-pandemic levels and has already maintained this level for several quarters now. That being said, I believe the current dividend is well-supported, with virtually no risk of a cut.

Valuation

The Seeking Alpha quant system gives the company a “B” grade for valuation. Notably, two metrics affect this rating—the company’s four-year average dividend yield and its price-to-book ratio (P/B ratio).

The company has received a “D+” on its average dividend yield over the past four years compared to its peers. However, I believe this metric is not relevant. Given that the company has restored its dividend for some time now, the more relevant metric would be the company’s forward dividend yield, which has a “B” grade from the quant system and better reflects the company’s current profile.

The company’s P/B ratio receives a “C+” grade, as it stands higher than its peers. However, a look at the company’s historical valuation trends shows that the current P/B ratio is actually the lowest it has been in the past 5 years. On a historical basis, the stock appears undervalued. This is supported by the fact that the company also trades at a discount to its own historical EV/EBITDA multiple, indicating that the current valuation may be cheap compared to its valuation over the past few years.

Risks

Despite its strengths, the company is not without its risks. The cyclical nature of the hospitality industry means the company remains sensitive to broader economic fluctuations and seasonal changes. Moreover, the company’s focus on luxury hotels means that the company could be more disproportionately affected by a recession, as luxury travel tends to be more discretionary in nature. That being said, the company’s strong balance sheet should allow it to withstand any downturn in the economy.

Conclusion

Host Hotels & Resorts has one of the strongest, if not the strongest, balance sheets among lodging REITs. The strength of its balance sheet has enabled the company to rebound from the pandemic, with its dividends returning to pre-pandemic levels when many other REITs have yet to do so. This is especially important as we are entering a period of economic uncertainty. The company’s dividend yield of over 5% is appealing to me, especially when I consider the strength of its balance sheet. As such, I rate the company a “Buy”.

data.title: Italian GDP Growth Confirmed At 0.3% In The First Quarter
data.description:
By Paolo Pizzoli

The second estimate of first-quarter GDP data, just released by Istat, confirmed that GDP growth was 0.3% in the first quarter (0.7% on the year) and provided a detailed breakdown of demand components.

On the supply side, Istat confirmed that value-added increased substantially in manufacturing and agriculture, while contracting marginally in services. The statistical carryover for 2025 GDP growth is 0.5%.

Gross fixed capital formation the main growth driver

The main driver of quarterly growth was domestic demand net of inventories, headed by gross fixed capital formation (+0.3% contribution), and helped by private consumption (+0.1% contribution). Interestingly, inventory change was the main drag, with a 0.3% negative contribution.

While private consumption growth was in line with our forecasts, gross fixed capital formation turned out stronger than expected, with all subcomponents posting positive growth. We highlight the improvement in the machinery component, in correspondence with the first positive value-added generation after several consecutive contractions.

It's also worth mentioning the gain in both sub-components in the investment domain. Beyond confirming the ongoing push on the infrastructural component of the spending of recovery funds, it also shows strong resilience in the residential component, irrespective of the end of the superbonus tax incentive.

Net exports, initially reported as a drag at the preliminary estimate stage, added 0.1% to quarterly growth, on an export drive, likely related to the acceleration of exports to the US in March in anticipation of the expected introduction of new tariffs in April.

A growth deceleration looks very likely in the second quarter

Like most of the big eurozone countries, Italy had a relatively strong start to 2025, marginally helped by exports with the US frontloading of imports in anticipation of tariffs. We believe a deceleration will be inevitable as the combined direct effect of higher tariffs and the indirect impact on confidence will eat into exports and, possibly, industrial investments.

The latest developments in the tariff saga, unfortunately, are not helping to alleviate uncertainty. A new judicial factor is now added to a complicated backdrop, where visibility on the state of negotiations between the US and the EU was already scarce. On a more positive note, the latest batch of confidence data points to a clear improvement among both consumers and service sector providers: a good omen for domestic demand.

All in all, against a very uncertain backdrop, we continue to believe that a slowdown in quarterly GDP growth will materialise in the second quarter and confirm our base case forecast of average Italian GDP growth at 0.6% in 2025.

data.title: Weitz Core Plus Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The Core Plus Income Fund’s Institutional Class returned +2.75% for the first quarter compared to a +2.78% return for the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (Agg). Longer-term results (3-, 5-, and 10-year) continue to outpace the index. The primary drivers of the Fund’s outperformance for the quarter were driven by sector and security selection, led by collateralized loan obligations (CLOs), asset-backed securities (ABS) and corporate bonds.

Overview

While any quarterly commentary will be stale by the time it is published, the first quarter of 2025 might be a particular case in point after the market turbulence brought about by the “Liberation Day” tariff news on April 2, 2025. For a post-quarter commentary of the market reaction to the news of early April, please read the first quarter Fixed Income Insights letter titled “Tariff Tantrum.” Now, on to the first quarter.

Interest rates, particularly shorter-term, declined during the first quarter of 2025 despite no interest rate moves by the Federal Reserve. 2-, 3-, and 5-year Treasury rates (the part of the yield curve that most impacts the Core Plus Income Fund) peaked in early January. They then proceeded to decline from that peak to quarter-end by one-quarter of one percent (5-year Treasuries) to as much as four-tenths of one percent (3-year Treasuries). See below for our standard view of the U.S. Treasury yield curve at three different moments in time (quarter-end, year-end, and one year before quarter-end).

As measured by credit spreads, risk assets widened (increased) during the quarter. Broad investment-grade spreads (as measured by the ICE BofA) increased by 15 basis points (bps) from 82 to 97 bps. High yield spreads (as measured by the ICE BofA) widened (increased) 61 basis points from 292 to 355 bps. After many years of declines, the quarter’s higher credit spreads were (and are) a welcome change. Higher credit spreads imply a potential improvement in the risk/reward environment for deploying capital in the Core Plus Income Fund.

Portfolio Positioning

The table on the next page shows the change in allocation to various sectors, from the prior quarter and from the prior year. This summary provides a view over time of how we have allocated capital. Since our goal is to invest in sectors that we believe offer the best risk-adjusted returns, our allocations may change significantly over time.

The most notable change in sector allocation during the first quarter was a 200 bps increase in commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS). CMBS activity was led by new issue single-asset, single-borrower (SASB) investments in the office and data center sectors. Our largest investment was in SKY1 2025-LINE, a SASB transaction collateralized by a single, floating rate, partially amortizing loan, secured by a first lien mortgage on a portfolio of six high quality, Class A, LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) Gold certified office properties located across four submarkets of the states of California and Washington. The properties are strategically located in technology-centric markets and have received a cumulative $125 million of capital expenditures since 2017 to remain competitive with updated amenity packages. Property tenants include investment-grade companies Amazon, Riot Games, GitHub, and Nestlé. In addition, we believe the transaction was structured with conservative leverage and strong debt service coverage. We expect to earn above-average returns over our 2-5 year investment horizon.

We also added two new rated feeder transactions from Brightwood and Guggenheim, consisting primarily of middle-market loans to private companies. In both transactions, we invested at the senior most portion of the capital structure. In addition, we added agency MBS, longer-duration Treasury securities, corporate bonds, and a wide variety of investments within ABS and commercial real estate (CRE) CLOs.

Portfolio Metrics

The Fund’s average effective maturity declined to 9.4 years as of March 31, 2025, from 9.5 years as of December 31, 2024, while our average effective duration decreased to 5.5 years from 5.8 years over the same period, compared to the Agg’s average effective duration of 6.0 years on March 31. These measures provide a guide to the Fund’s interest rate sensitivity. A higher average effective duration increases the Fund’s price sensitivity to changes in interest rates (either up or down).

As of March 31, our high-yield exposure as a percentage of net assets was 4.7%, up from 3.9% on December 31, 2024. The Fund can invest up to 25% of net assets in high yield, therefore we have ample capacity to take advantage of valuation discrepancies/ opportunities in the high yield area.

The Fund’s yield to worst (YTW) remains higher than the Agg, while the Fund’s interest rate risk (duration) continues to be lower. Constructing a portfolio that produces “carry” – income achieved from regular coupon payments – (via the YTW measurement) and lower interest rate sensitivity (via lower duration) is a feature of our flexible mandate and ability to invest in other higher quality securities that reside outside of the benchmark. The Fund’s 30-day SEC yield was 5.07% (subsidized) and 5.00% (unsubsidized) as of March 31, 2025 (subsidized yield reflects fee waivers and/or expense reimbursements, while unsubsidized yield does not).

Top Quarterly Contributors

  • U.S. Treasury Bonds: U.S. Treasuries were the largest contributor to performance. With a duration of over 10 years, our Treasury portfolio experienced unrealized mark-to-market gains as interest rates declined across the curve during the quarter.
  • Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS): Agency MBS was the second largest contributor to performance as lower interest rates and carry more than offset wider mortgage-backed spreads during the quarter.
  • Asset Backed Securities: Our ABS portfolio was a positive contributor to performance due to high coupon income and price appreciation, as ABS spreads tightened throughout the year.

Top Quarterly Detractors

There were no material detractors to fund performance during the first quarter.

Fund Strategy Revisited

Our investment approach consists primarily of investing in a diversified portfolio of high-quality bonds while maintaining an overall portfolio average duration of 3.5 to 7 years. When attractively priced, we may seek to capture attractive coupon income and potential price appreciation by investing in longer-duration and lower-quality bonds. We may also invest up to 25% in fixed income securities that are not considered investment grade (such as high yield and convertible bonds as well as preferred and convertible preferred stock), and we do so when we perceive the risk/reward characteristics to be favorable.

We do not, and will not, try to mimic any index as we construct our portfolio. We believe our flexible mandate and high-conviction portfolio will benefit investors long term. We utilize a bottom-up, research-driven approach and select portfolio assets one security at a time based on our view of opportunities in the marketplace. Our fixed income research is not dependent on, but often benefits from, the due diligence work our equity teammates conduct on companies and industries.

Overall, we strive to be adequately compensated for the risks assumed to maximize investment (or reinvestment) yield and to avoid making interest rate bets, particularly those that depend on interest rates going down. We have often maintained a lower duration profile than the index, particularly in very low-yield environments. Our shorter duration profile has benefited shareholders in periods of rising interest rates.

Maintaining a liquid (e.g., Treasuries and agency MBS) and overall, up-in-quality portfolio positioning that we believe can generate higher income returns than respective indexes positions us well as we begin a new year–a year that could get messy in so many unpredictable ways. But it could also be one that will particularly allow us to lean into and be liquidity providers if/when more favorable risk/reward opportunities present themselves.

We are thankful for the trust and partnership many have in our firm and its investment process.

data.title: John Hancock Freedom 529 Portfolio 2029-2032 Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary1 Highlights

  • In the first quarter of 2025, the bellwether S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) recorded a mid-single-digit decline after posting yet another fresh all-time high in mid February.
  • Postelection optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, especially related to trade and tariffs.
  • The Fed kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024.

Market review and outlook

In the first quarter of 2025, the bellwether S&P 500 Index recorded a mid-single digit decline after posting yet another fresh all-time high in mid-February. Postelection optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, disruption to government programs, and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities, especially related to trade and tariffs. Amid rising volatility, the index gained 2.78% in January before falling in February (−1.30%) and March (-5.63%). It was particularly dragged down by growth stocks and small-cap stocks.

During the quarter, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024. The central bank indicated that further reductions would depend on real progress on inflation or unexpected weakness in labor. New data showed that inflation climbed 2.80% in February from a year earlier-higher than the Fed's 2.00% target-as underlying price pressure intensified. Meanwhile, confidence and sentiment among U.S. consumers continued to slip, mostly reflecting a gloomier outlook for inflation and the economy's path ahead.

Against this backdrop, the S&P 500 Index returned -4.27% in the first quarter. However, international developed-market stocks gained 6.94%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index, buoyed partly by strength in Germany ( +16.00% ), where February brought elections and optimism that the new administration led by Friedrich Merz would pursue a pro-growth agenda. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 2.93% advance. Some emerging European markets, including Poland (+31%), Greece (+23%), and the Czech Republic (+29%), posted strong returns, bolstered by an improved outlook for the eurozone following Germany's plans to boost spending on defense and infrastructure. China (+15%) was also a standout performer; however, Taiwan (-13%) and India (-3.00%) were relatively weak.

Investment-grade bonds rebounded during the quarter as U.S. Treasury yields eased. Long-term Treasuries and Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities did particularly well amid growing economic growth and inflation concerns. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index rose 2.78%.

Almost every measure of consumer and business sentiment, or soft data, has notably deteriorated amid rising uncertainty around trade policy. While sentiment usually lags hard data, such as employment and spending, today's soft data signals a far more dire outlook than the hard data implies. The real concern is whether businesses and consumers will continue to face this level of uncertainty for a prolonged period. With trade disputes lasting for nearly a year and a half during President Trump's first term, the sentiment could be right this time around, foreshadowing a deeper economic slowdown. Given the heightened risk, we continued to lower U.S. equity exposure while adding to European equities on an upside potential related to Germany's increased fiscal spending on defense and infrastructure and a more unified commitment from leaders across the eurozone on improving competitiveness.

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio (MUTF:JENAX) recorded a modest gain for the quarter and outpaced its blended benchmark, comprising a 38.50% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index, a 16.50% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net), and a 45.00% weighting in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. An out-of-benchmark stake in the Real Assets Fund (T. Rowe Price) notably contributed for the quarter, as this asset class outperformed core equities, driven by the precious metals and energy groups. Exposure to non-U.S. stocks also added value in a period during which U.S. equities lagged. International Growth Fund (John Hancock) was a key relative contributor, aided by security selection in the industrials sector. Equity Income Fund (T. Rowe Price) also provided a relative performance boost, largely on the strength of security selection within industrials and consumer staples. On the other hand, Spectrum Income Fund (T. Rowe Price) detracted, mainly due to exposure to high-yield bonds, U.S. dollar-hedged international bonds, and floating-rate loans.

data.title: VNQ: If You Think REITs Are Safe, It's Time To Rethink (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
VNQ ETF: Previous thesis and new developments

My last analysis on the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VNQ) was published back in October 2024. In an article entitled “VNQ: This Chart Shows It's Not Too Late To Buy REITs,” I argued for a buy thesis based on its high dividend yield at that time. More specifically, I argued that:

The REIT sector has recently outperformed the SP500 by a large margin. However, VNQ's most recent dividend payouts suggest its price advancements have yet to catch up with earnings growth. VNQ's latest quarterly dividend distribution made in October translates into a 50% YOY growth when returned capital is excluded and 27% growth on a TTM basis. VNQ is currently still trading at its most attractive levels in a decade and remains the top ranked sector fund on our radar.

Since then, there have been a few material changes to our macroscopic landscape. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on the top one that is most relevant to VNQ and also REIT stocks in general: the likelihood of persisting high interest rates. Due to a series of developments ranging from inflation data and also the ongoing trade disputes, interest rates have increased substantially since my last writing.

Take 10-year treasury rates (US10Y) as an example. The reading has increased from about 3.75% at the time of my last analysis to the current level of around 4.4%. In early May, when Moody’s Ratings announced the downgrade of the U.S. credit rating, 10-year rates pushed above 4.5%. Rates with longer durations increased even more with 30-year rates temporarily peaking above 5%.

Under these conditions, the market now expects large odds for interest rates to stay elevated, making REIT funds like VNQ untimely due to their holding’s reliance on debt financing. These considerations have also caused me to downgrade my rating on VNQ to hold, as detailed next.

VNQ ETF: Interest rate outlook

Looking ahead, the market’s current expectation points to far higher odds for interest rates to stay elevated in the near future. To be more explicit, the next chart is provided by CME Group FedWatch Tool, which shows the current probability of interest rate cuts for the rest of 2025. As seen, currently, the most likely scenario that the market expects is that they will lower rates to the 3.75% to 4.00% range by the end of the year. And the expected probability is 39.8%. Compared to the expectations only a month ago, this new outlook is far less aggressive. A month ago, the market expected deeper cuts to the range of 3.25% to 3.5% range, with a higher probability of 41.6%.

VNQ ETF: Basic facts

With the overall background provided thus far, the rest of the discussion will be more specific to VNQ. Thus, let me first cover some basics of the fund to facilitate the rest of the analysis. VNQ is one of the largest and also most popular ETFs with targeted exposure to publicly traded REIT stocks as detailed in its fund description below:

VNQ fund description: The fund’s objective is to provide a high level of income and moderate long-term capital appreciation by tracking the performance of a benchmark index that measures the performance of publicly traded equity REITs and other real estate-related investments. The benchmark index is the MSCI US Investable Market Real Estate 25/50 Index. The fund is passively managed and uses a full-replication approach. The fund features low expenses to minimize net tracking error.

The fund’s indexing method is clearly reflected in its top holdings as seen below. Its largest holdings represent broad exposure to various sub-sectors ranging from telecommunication towers, storage, malls, etc. To wit, its largest 4 individual holdings are American Tower Corp. (accounting for 6.25% of holdings), Prologis, Inc. (5.61%), Welltower Inc. (5.52%), and Equinix, Inc. (4.79%).

VNQ ETF: Downturn performance

Given VNQ’s focus on REIT stocks as discussed above, many investors consider VNQ as a “safer” investment. There are certainly good reasons here (and I will elaborate more on this a bit later). But I urge investors to reexamine this assumption under current conditions. I believe the developments since my last writing have made a market downturn more likely.

Historically, VNQ has suffered deeper and longer drawdowns than the S&P 500 (SP500) (SPY), as you can see from the following two charts below. There are a couple of reasons that can help us understand this. The top one on my list is again leveraged, especially during times with elevated borrowing rates as aforementioned. Real estate properties are an asset class that often involves higher leverage than the overall economy. Leverage, of course, amplifies returns in good times, but also magnifies losses during downturns. A second reason that can make VNQ suffer larger drawdowns than SP500 is the perception of liquidity. The major assets of REIT stocks are physical properties, which are often less liquid, especially during market downturns, compared to other types of assets.

Other risks and final thoughts

Besides the issue of borrowing rates, a couple of other risks are worth mentioning. As aforementioned, VNQ is one of the largest and most popular REIT funds, as I know. As you can see from the following chart, VNQ has a net expense ratio of 0.13% and approximately $63.1 billion in total net assets, far exceeding its peers. However, a few competing funds are becoming worthy alternatives in terms of expense ratios and assets under management (AUM). Notably, the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE) features an expense ratio of 0.09%, significantly lower than VNQ. With a substantial AUM of $7.28 billion, XLRE also offers sufficient liquidity to meet most investors’ needs. The Fidelity MSCI Real Estate Index ETF (FREL) boasts a very competitive expense ratio of 0.08% and also manages a sizable AUM of $1.04 billion in assets.

To echo a comment made earlier, there are good reasons that many investors consider VNQ as a “safer” investment. The leading factor is the generous dividend payouts. As you can see from the following chart, currently, the dividend yield of VNQ stands at 4.07%, a quite attractive level by either horizontal (say compared to treasury rates) or vertical comparison (i.e., compared to its own historical levels). For example, in the past 10 years, the average historical yield of VNQ is 3.99%. The current yield is thus slightly above the historical average, indicating an attractive valuation. Also note that despite some extreme data points, VNQ's dividend yield has largely fluctuated with a relatively narrow range in the long term between about 2.5% and 4.5%, an indicator of the fund’s stability.

To recap, this revisit to VNQ is triggered by the change in our interest rate outlook since my last writing. Due to a series of geopolitical and economic events since then, interest rates have increased substantially since my last writing and are likely to remain elevated. I expect these changes to pressure REIT funds like VNQ and suggest investors reexamine their assumption of their downside protection should a market downturn materialize. These considerations have led me to lower my rating on VNQ to hold under current conditions.
